title: Handwriting and Spatial Script Practice
subtitle: Block printing, shorthand, cursive forms, and ordinary signed writing
summary: Native anchor paper for the Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting series on handwriting, material practice, and script culture in SignWriting.
synopsis: This entry argues that a writing system is not only a formal model, font-rendered output, or machine-readable text stream. It also becomes writing through ordinary practice: handwriting, abbreviation, learner forms, personal variation, correction, reuse, and material habits. It connects SignWriting handwriting to grapholinguistic questions of materiality, treats block, shorthand, and cursive traditions as important but under-documented evidence for spatial script practice, and identifies the research needed to understand handwritten signed text more fully.

slides.title: Writing In Hands
slides.summary: A writing system is not only a formal model or font-rendered output; it also becomes writing through ordinary material practice.

image_prompts.concrete: Scholarly slide showing handwritten SignWriting notes, block forms, shorthand marks, correction, and reusable written practice
image_prompts.abstract: A formal sign becoming ordinary handwritten practice

slides.speech: This paper argues that writing is not only a formal model, font output, or machine-readable stream. A writing system also becomes real through handwriting, abbreviation, learner forms, correction, reuse, and material habits.
slides.notes: Open by grounding theory in practice.

slides.title: Block, Shorthand, Cursive
slides.summary: Block printing, shorthand, and cursive traditions are important but underdocumented evidence for spatial script practice.

image_prompts.concrete: Comparison slide showing block SignWriting, shorthand forms, cursive movement, and printed forms as related handwriting practices
image_prompts.abstract: Three handwriting styles showing different degrees of compression and fluency

slides.speech: The paper treats block printing, shorthand, and cursive forms as important evidence. They show that SignWriting has material practice, but that practice is still underdocumented and needs more careful study.
slides.notes: Do not overclaim the handwriting record.

slides.title: Variation And Legibility
slides.summary: Handwriting introduces variation, but variation is normal in writing systems when legibility and correction practices are stable enough.

image_prompts.concrete: Writing systems slide showing multiple handwritten variants of a sign converging on legible shared structure
image_prompts.abstract: Variable handwritten marks held together by readable convention

slides.speech: Variation is not automatically a weakness. All handwriting varies. The question is whether writers and readers share enough distinctions for legibility, correction, teaching, and reuse.
slides.notes: This keeps handwriting inside the writing-systems argument.

slides.title: Research Agenda
slides.summary: The handwriting record should become more visible through documentation, examples, reader response, and study of ordinary practice.

image_prompts.concrete: Research agenda slide for SignWriting handwriting with documentation, classroom practice, personal notes, reader response, and corpus examples
image_prompts.abstract: Handwritten signs becoming documented research material

slides.speech: The conclusion is a research agenda. SignWriting has a strong technical record. The handwriting record needs to become more visible through examples, teaching documentation, reader response work, and connections to the formal model.
slides.notes: Close as an invitation to documentation and research.

slides.title: Known And Open
slides.summary: The paper treats handwriting as real writing practice while keeping the scale, variation, reader response, and corpus questions open.

image_prompts.concrete: Research boundary slide showing known SignWriting handwriting practice on one side and open questions about scale, variation, reader response, teaching, and corpora on the other
image_prompts.abstract: Handwritten spatial signs becoming a documented but still open research field

slides.speech: The paper should land with both confidence and restraint. Handwriting and material practice are real parts of writing. At the same time, the scale of practice, the range of variation, reader response, teaching habits, and corpus evidence still need better documentation.
slides.notes: This is the boundary version of the research agenda. It prevents the handwriting claim from sounding more complete than the evidence.
